root.title: A Minto Pyramid deserves a dedicated workspace

children.title: A Minto Pyramid is a sign of respect
Time: You value the audience's time by getting to the point
The most important finding as the leading phrase
children.body: — No explanations — No setting the scene — Absolutely no click-baits

children.title: Like old-fashioned journalism
children.body: Man bites dog — Warm and sunny — National innovation prize to local farmer

children.title: Intellect: You trust the audience to connect the dots
Traditional
children.body: — Here's the market... — Here's the data... — Here's the analysis... — Here's the conclusion...

children.title: Minto
children.body: — Market share is declining because churn has doubled — Here's why

children.title: Judgement: You trust the audience to determine how much detail they need
An exec-summary built in the structure is quite smart
Readers can stop when they are satisfied
Appendix 7 is not for everyone
A Minto Pyramid is foremost a thinking framework
Focus — From the outset, look for the key finding
The Minto Pyramid demands dicipline
Eye on the prize. From the start
You are not collecting facts, you are searching for an answer
Clarity — From the outset, strive for precise arguments and robust logic
Everything in the document has to support it’s purpose
"I have made this letter longer than usual because I have not had time to make it shorter.“ — Blaise Pascal, 1657
Analysis is a means to an end, not the end itself
Ownership — prepare for exposing your findings to scrutiny
It takes a lot of courage to expose your finding right at the start — No cover — No excuses
A finding that you are comfortable to put front-and-center is a message on its own — ”I did the work” — ”This is what I think”
The Minto Pyramid doesn’t translate well to PowerPoint
The Minto Pyramid is a layered structure, while PowerPoint follows a sequential logic
children.body: A well structured pyramid becomes a repetitive mess when forced to slides

children.title: Minto Pyramid guides the thought — PowerPoint records thoughts
Minto
children.body: ”Is this relevant?” — ”Does this support the claim?” — ”Is the claim robust enough?”

children.title: PowerPoint
children.body: ”I can always decrease the font size” — ”I can add a slide for this” — ”This is an interesting piece of information”

children.title: Minto Pyramid gives power to the audience — PowerPoint keeps power with the presenter
Minto Pyramid supports non-linear consumption of information
children.body:
”Where do you want to go next?”

”How do you want to spend the next ten minutes?”
”What do you want to explore in more detail?”

children.title: PowerPoint supports linear storytelling
children.body:
The presenter decides the order in advance - Audience input is difficult to take into account - 
Skipping breaks the rhythm

pool.title: From the outset, prepare for exposing your findings to scrutiny
A Minto Pyramid deserves a dedicated workspace
Minto Pyramid — Gives power to the audience — Is a layered structure — Guides the thought
PowerPoint — Keeps power with the presenter — Follows a sequential logic — Records thoughts
Present the diamond, not the excavation
prepare for exposing your findings to scrutiny
